entities.cite: those words that are to be explained

entities.cite: ancillary to dharma and to cognition

axioms.name: apoddhāra
axioms.cite: the word is not a natural unit given prior to the sentence but is *extracted* from it by analysis

axioms.name: vyakhyāyate
axioms.cite: the anvakhyeya/pratipadaka distinction between what gets explained and what does the explaining

axioms.name: kāryakāraṇa-bhāva
axioms.cite: relations of two kinds, causal and of yogyata or fitness

axioms.name: yogya-bhāva
axioms.cite: relations of two kinds, causal and of yogyata or fitness

denials.name: sarva-upavarnita
denials.cite: not everything true of these matters is transmitted, only kecid eva, some only

claims.name: sthita-lakṣaṇa
claims.cite: meanings whose defining marks are fixed

claims.name: vyakhyāyate
claims.cite: words that are to be explained, and those that do the explaining

claims.name: kāryakāraṇa-bhāva
claims.cite: relations obtaining by way of cause and effect

claims.name: yogya-bhāva
claims.cite: relations obtaining by way of cause and effect and by way of fitness

rejected_readings.label: word_as_primitive
rejected_readings.rendering: Words are primitive linguistic units from which sentences are built.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the presupposed apoddhara doctrine: the verse grounds itself in the principle that words are extracted from the sentence by analysis, not given as natural prior units. The sentence is the real linguistic whole.

rejected_readings.label: comprehensive_transmission
rejected_readings.rendering: This science transmits every true matter concerning meanings and relations.
rejected_readings.why: The final clause of the verse explicitly denies this: only some matters are transmitted according to tradition, not all. The discipline is selective.

notes: Verse 1.24-26 catalogues the Vākyapadīya's subject matter, presupposing the apoddhara doctrine (word-extraction from sentence). The commentary establishes core axioms: abstracted meanings, fixed-mark meanings, the anvakhyeya/pratipadaka distinction, and two types of relations (causal and fitness-based). The verse adds their ancillary role with respect to dharma and cognition. The selective transmission principle reflects Bhartrhari's realism about disciplinary transmission.
